title: Difference Array
description: Prefix Sum's mirror image: O(1) range updates instead of O(1) range queries, reconstructed with a prefix sum at the end.
objectives: Understand the problem difference arrays solve: many range updates
Apply a range update in O(1) using a difference array
Reconstruct the final array using a prefix sum
See the direct relationship between this and Prefix Sum

sections.text: Prefix Sum made many range queries fast on a fixed array. Difference Array solves the mirror-image problem: making many range updates fast, when you only need the final result at the end.
A difference array stores, at each index, the difference between that element and the previous one — letting a range update be applied in O(1) by marking only its two boundaries.
4. Applying a Range Update in O(1)

diff[l] += val marks "starting here, add val." diff[r + 1] -= val marks "starting here, stop adding it." Neither line touches the range's actual elements — each update is just two array writes, O(1), no matter how large the range is.

This is exactly the prefix sum computation from Prefix Sum, applied to the difference array instead of the original one. Taking the prefix sum of a difference array "undoes" the differencing and reveals the final, fully-updated values.

sections.title: Verifying it
points: Indices 1-3: +5 from the first update → indices 1,2,3 get +5
Indices 2-5: +2 from the second update → indices 2,3,4,5 get +2
Index 1: 5. Index 2: 5+2=7. Index 3: 5+2=7. Index 4: 2. Index 5: 2 — matches 0 5 7 7 2 2

sections.title: Marking a calendar's start and end dates instead of filling in every day
sections.text: Booking a week-long vacation doesn't require writing "on vacation" on all seven individual calendar pages one at a time — you could just note "starts here" on day 1 and "ends here" on day 8. Marking many separate trips this way, then reading through the calendar once at the very end to see the final combined schedule, is exactly the difference array technique.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting the difference array needs one extra slot beyond the original array's size, for the r + 1 boundary marker.
Applying updates directly to the real array when many range updates are needed — defeats the purpose; mark boundaries in the difference array instead.
Forgetting to reconstruct the final array with a prefix sum pass at the end — the difference array alone isn't the answer, it's an intermediate representation.
Off-by-one errors on the r + 1 boundary — it marks where the effect STOPS, one past the last affected index.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What problem does a difference array solve?
questions.answer: Applying many range updates to an array efficiently, deferring the actual per-element work until a single final reconstruction pass.

questions.question: How do you apply a range update, adding val to indices l through r, using a difference array?
questions.answer: diff[l] += val; diff[r + 1] -= val; — marking where the effect starts and where it stops, without touching the range's individual elements.

questions.question: How do you get the final array from a difference array?
questions.answer: Take its prefix sum — the same running-total computation as Prefix Sum, applied to the difference array.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of applying k range updates and then reconstructing the final array, using a difference array?
questions.answer: O(k) for the updates (O(1) each) plus O(n) for the final reconstruction, versus O(k × range length) for naive direct updates.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A difference array is the mirror image of prefix sum: instead of making range queries fast, it makes range updates fast. Adding a value to every index from l to r becomes just two O(1) writes — diff[l] += val and diff[r + 1] -= val — marking where the effect starts and stops, without touching any of the range's actual elements. After all updates are applied, taking the prefix sum of the difference array reconstructs the final, fully-updated array in O(n). This turns k potentially expensive range updates into k O(1) operations plus one O(n) pass at the end.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Difference Array
sections.text: Difference array = mark 'starts here' and 'stops here' on a calendar, don't fill in every day. Prefix-sum the difference array at the end to reveal the real schedule.

sections.title: Corporate Flight Bookings
sections.problemStatement: There are n flights numbered 1 to n. bookings[i] = [first_i, last_i, seats_i] means seats_i seats were booked on every flight from first_i to last_i inclusive. Return an array answer of length n where answer[i] is the total seats booked on flight i+1.
hints: Each booking is a RANGE UPDATE (add seats_i to every flight from first_i to last_i) — exactly this lesson's core problem.
Don't loop over every flight in each booking's range — mark the range's two boundaries in a difference array instead.
After marking every booking's boundaries, one prefix-sum pass reconstructs the total seats per flight.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For every booking, loop across its entire flight range and add seats directly to the answer array.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(bookings × range length), up to O(n²)

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Mark each booking's two boundaries in a 1-indexed difference array, then reconstruct with a single prefix-sum pass.

sections.title: Car Pooling
sections.problemStatement: A car has a given capacity. trips[i] = [numPassengers, from, to] means numPassengers get picked up at location from and dropped off at location to. Return true if the car can complete all trips without exceeding capacity at any point.

examples.explanation: At location 3, both trips overlap: 2+3=5 passengers > capacity 4.

hints: Each trip is a RANGE UPDATE on 'number of passengers currently in the car' across locations from to to-1 (the passenger is gone by location 'to').
Mark +numPassengers at 'from' and -numPassengers at 'to' in a difference array — the passenger's drop-off point should NOT get the +.
A single prefix-sum pass over locations gives the passenger count at every point — check whether it ever exceeds capacity.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For every trip, loop across every location it covers and add its passengers directly to a location-count array.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Mark +passengers at 'from' and -passengers at 'to' in a difference array, then check the running total never exceeds capacity in one pass.

sections.title: Range Addition
sections.problemStatement: Given an array of length n initialized to 0, and a list of updates [start, end, inc] where each adds inc to every element from start to end inclusive, return the final array after all updates.
hints: This IS the exact problem this lesson's Section 6 example demonstrates — apply each update as two boundary marks in a difference array.
diff[start] += inc marks where the addition begins; diff[end + 1] -= inc marks where it stops.
After every update is marked, one prefix-sum pass gives the final array directly.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For every update, loop across its entire range and add inc directly to the result array.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Mark each update's two boundaries in a difference array, then reconstruct the final array with a single prefix-sum pass.

sections.title: Zero Array Transformation I
sections.problemStatement: Given nums and queries where each query [l, r] decrements every element in nums[l..r] by at most 1 (values clamp at 0, never go negative), return true if it's possible for nums to become all zeros after processing every query in order.
hints: Since each query decrements by exactly 1 and clamps at 0, index i can be reduced to 0 in total by however many queries COVER index i — order doesn't matter for the final feasibility check.
Counting how many queries cover each index i is a range-update problem — this lesson's difference array technique applies directly.
Build a difference array over the queries (mark +1 at l, -1 at r+1 for each query), prefix-sum it to get each index's total coverage count, then check coverage[i] >= nums[i] for every i.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For every query, loop its range and increment a coverage counter per index directly.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Mark each query's boundaries in a difference array (O(1) per query), then reconstruct coverage counts with one prefix-sum pass and compare against nums.

sections.title: Zero Array Transformation II
sections.problemStatement: Given nums and queries where queries[i] = [l, r, val] decrements every element in nums[l..r] by AT MOST val (clamped at 0), return the minimum number of queries (taken as a prefix of the list, in order) needed so nums can become all zeros — or -1 if even all queries together aren't enough.

examples.explanation: Using only the first query isn't enough (index 0 only gets 1 of the 2 it needs); the first two queries together give index 0 a total of 2, which is enough for every index.
Even using both queries, index 0 only ever receives 1 total decrement, but nums[0] = 4.

hints: For a FIXED number of queries k (using the first k in order), the question 'can nums become zero?' is answered exactly like Zero Array Transformation I — build a difference array from those k queries' [l, r, val] and check total coverage at each index is >= nums[i].
Using more queries never makes feasibility WORSE (coverage can only grow) — feasibility is monotonic in k, which is the classic signal for binary search on the answer (recall Binary Search).
Binary search over k from 0 to queries.size(): for each candidate k, run the O(k + n) feasibility check from hint 1, and narrow toward the smallest feasible k.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Try every possible k from 0 upward, rebuilding the difference array from scratch each time and checking feasibility, stopping at the first k that works.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(q × (q + n)) — rebuilds the diff array from scratch at every k

approaches.label: Better
approaches.explanation: Maintain the difference array incrementally, adding only the NEW query at each step k instead of rebuilding from scratch — still checks every k linearly, but avoids redundant rebuilding.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(q × n) — one O(n) feasibility check per candidate k

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Feasibility only improves as k grows (more queries add non-negative coverage), so binary search directly for the smallest feasible k instead of checking every k linearly.
